cataloging_subject.label: The Desert of Religion
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English devotional poem conventionally called The Desert of Religion, as transmitted in three illustrated British Library manuscripts and represented by Walter Hübner's two-part 1911 edition and University of Michigan CME item CME00075. The record treats the poem as one intellectual work with a compilatory textual and visual design. It retains separately modeled German editorial framing, manuscript criticism, and source analysis because the XML includes them, but it does not confuse those modern prose sections with the medieval poem, any one codex with the work as a whole, or Richard Rolle, Walter Hilton, John Alcock, the Speculum vitae, and other sources with secure authorship of the complete work.

catalog_summary.title: The Desert of Religion
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English author-compiler
catalog_summary.editor: Walter Hübner, with textual and linguistic commentary by K. Schreiner
catalog_summary.date: Fifteenth-century composition or compilation; earliest surviving attestation window in the first half of the century; first published in 1911
catalog_summary.region: Northern England for dialect and manuscript tradition; exact place of composition unresolved

titles.supplied_by: Walter Hübner in 1911 and retained by modern scholarship
notes: Mouron explicitly says Hübner gave the work this title. It is an established editorial title, not a verified medieval rubric.

titles.value: The desert of religion. Mit dem Bilde des Richard Rolle of Hampole
notes: The mixed English-German source title highlights the Richard Rolle image but does not attribute the entire poem to Rolle.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English author-compiler of The Desert of Religion
notes: A descriptive identity for an unknown author-compiler. Mouron treats a monastic and possibly Carthusian context as probable or possible, not proved personal identity.

agents.name: K. Schreiner
notes: The edition supplies only an initial and surname.

agents.name: Walter Hilton
notes: Richard James's historical Cotton attribution was repeated in early catalogs; Frederick Madden and Hübner reject it as likely confusion with Hilton's prose work on contemplative life.

agents.name: John Alcock
notes: Thomas Astle's eighteenth-century volume-wide attribution in Stowe MS 39 is explicitly called incorrect by the current British Library catalog.

assertion.notes: The poem identifies itself as taken from many books; Allen and Mouron document extensive compilation from named sources.

scope.description: Hübner's two-part 1911 edition as represented by CME00075

assertion.notes: The edition supplies A as its base and C/S readings throughout; the exact division of physical collation labor is not stated.

scope.description: Historical Cotton attribution, not accepted authorship

assertion.notes: Supported only as attribution history. Madden and Hübner call the authorship claim very doubtful.

scope.description: Astle's later attribution of the Stowe volume's Middle English texts

assertion.notes: The current British Library catalog explicitly calls the attribution incorrect.

date_statements.display: Fifteenth century in modern specialist description; exact compilation date unresolved

assertion.notes: Mouron's modern description calls the text fifteenth-century; the surviving witnesses establish an upper bound but do not pinpoint one act of composition or compilation.

date_statements.display: Earliest surviving attestation window in the first half of the fifteenth century: Stowe MS 39 is dated 1400-1449 and Cotton part II 1425-1449

assertion.notes: The overlapping catalog ranges establish the earliest surviving attestation window verified here but do not prove whether Stowe or Cotton is physically the earlier copy.

date_statements.display: 1911, first published by Walter Hübner in two installments within Archiv volume 126

assertion.notes: Hübner's subtitle says the poem is published for the first time; part I prints the text and part II supplies criticism and source study later in the same volume.

places.label: Northern England
notes: A discovery region for the poem's language and the manuscripts' shared northern tradition, not an exact settlement or proven composition site.

assertion.notes: Schreiner argues from rhyme, morphology, and vocabulary for Northern language. His conditional suggestion of Scotland from one rhyme is not normalized as a place claim.

scope.description: Quoted witness readings, poem lines, and captions inside editorial prose

form_statements.description: The cataloged medieval work is a rhyming-couplet poem. The substantial German prose is retained editorial framing and scholarship rather than part of the medieval expression.
German manuscript description with an extended English quotation from Frederick Madden.
Northern Middle English rhyming couplets arranged around a prologue, twenty conceptual tree sections, and an epilogue.
German prose commentary incorporating fourteen separately encoded Middle English verse lines and a textualized shield-of-faith diagram.
German textual and linguistic analysis with verse readings and a manuscript stemma.
German source commentary with substantial Latin quotations.

assertion.notes: The poem reorganizes doctrinal lists and borrowed passages into one illustrated wilderness-and-tree design.

subject_statements.label: Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments
Visual devotion, saints, hermits, and tree diagrams

summaries.value: The Desert of Religion is an anonymous Northern Middle English devotional poem that turns religious life into a spiritual wilderness and forest. Its rhyming couplets organize catechetical and ascetic teaching—virtues and vices, humility and pride, commandments, sacraments, works of mercy, confession, contemplation, and the perils of temptation—around twenty allegorical trees. In all three surviving fifteenth-century manuscripts, tree diagrams and images of saints and hermits form an integral visual program that invites meditative reading. The author-compiler openly says the treatise was taken from many books; Hope Emily Allen and Anne Mouron identify the Speculum vitae as its chief source alongside The Prick of Conscience, Richard Rolle's Emendatio vitae, and other devotional materials. CME00075 represents Hübner's 1911 edited poem and apparatus together with his German manuscript description and afterword, K. Schreiner's German textual and linguistic criticism, and Hübner's German source study; those editorial prose sections are retained context, not medieval authorship.

The XML encodes Hübner's Archiv 126 pp. 58-74 and the same volume's pp. 360-364. It contains a four-paragraph manuscript preface, a 940-line main poem with extensive footnote apparatus, an afterword with fourteen quoted verse lines and a shield diagram, Schreiner's manuscript and language criticism with a stemma, and Hübner's source study. It omits two unpaginated manuscript-image plates and their blank backs; the complete cached volume preserves those surfaces.

The medieval poem has a prologue, twenty conceptual tree sections, and an epilogue in Mouron's description. The encoded editorial unit surrounds it with Hübner's descriptions of Add MS 37049, Cotton Faustina B VI part II, and Stowe 39; discussion of Richard Rolle portraits and captions; Schreiner's stemma and Northern-language analysis; and Hübner's biblical, patristic, and allegorical source discussion.

The preferred title is Hübner's supplied 1911 title. The anonymous poem is not assigned to Walter Hilton, John Alcock, or Richard Rolle. Hübner's 940-line base text and Mouron's 943-line modern lineation are preserved as edition-specific extents pending direct reconciliation.

extent.description: The XML's five stable intellectual/editorial parts are modeled below. It contains 19 LG and 954 L elements: 940 main-poem lines plus 14 verse lines quoted in Hübner's afterword. Mouron's 2016 article describes a 943-line poem divided conceptually into 22 sections (prologue, twenty trees, epilogue); that modern edition-level lineation is not substituted for the reproducible XML count.

content_parts.label: Hübner's manuscript description
content_parts.summary: Four German prose paragraphs identifying and describing the three witnesses, with Frederick Madden's English note rejecting the Cotton Walter Hilton attribution.

content_parts.label: The Desert of Religion
content_parts.summary: Hübner's 940-line critical base text of the anonymous Middle English devotional poem, with A readings in the continuous stream and extensive C/S variants in footnotes.
notes: The XML's 19 verse groups do not encode Mouron's 22 conceptual sections one-for-one.
The visual program occupies facing and accompanying manuscript surfaces; these text-only XML metrics do not measure all inscriptions in the manuscript images.

content_parts.label: Hübner's afterword on the illustrated tradition
content_parts.summary: German prose discussing the three manuscripts' images, Richard Rolle identifications and captions, and the relationship between the poem and its illustrated saints and trees, with fourteen quoted Middle English verse lines and a shield diagram.

content_parts.label: Schreiner's manuscript criticism, rhyme, and language study
content_parts.summary: German prose arguing that no surviving witness directly copies another, that C and S share an exemplar, that A has the fewest errors and should be the base, and that the poem's language is Northern.
notes: Schreiner's Scotland suggestion is explicitly conditional and is not promoted into a confirmed composition place.

content_parts.label: Hübner's source study
content_parts.summary: German prose identifying biblical and patristic analogues, Jacobus de Voragine, allegorical desert and tree traditions, and the poem's stated compilation from many books.
notes: Allen's later addendum substantially expands and corrects the source analysis by demonstrating extensive direct borrowing from the Speculum vitae.

related_works.title: Speculum vitae
notes: Allen maps extensive direct quotations and calls it the main source; Mouron likewise identifies it as the chief source. The physical Cambridge manuscript Allen used for comparison is not claimed to be the author-compiler's exemplar.

related_works.title: The Prick of Conscience
notes: Allen maps selected line ranges and Mouron identifies it among the poem's sources; this is a passage-level dependency, not a claim that the entire poem adapts only this work.

related_works.title: Emendatio vitae
notes: The twelve degrees of perfect living derive, with one exception, from Richard Rolle's Emendatio vitae. This source relationship and the Rolle portraits do not make Rolle author of The Desert of Religion.

related_works.title: De duodecim abusionum gradibus
notes: Allen proposes this sometimes Augustinian or Cyprian work as the probable source behind the poem's reference to twelve abuses, but notes that The Desert does not enumerate them. The dependency remains qualified.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME source XML for CME00075
evidence.locator: IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1 and DIV2 structure; poem, apparatus, afterword, and commentary
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00075, the Hübner title, Middle English poem, German editorial matter, Latin quotations, contributor signatures, witness sigla, source page milestones, and related attribution history.

evidence.citation: Local structural inspection of CME00075.xml and comparison with the complete Archiv 126 scan
evidence.locator: 2 DIV1; 5 DIV2; 19 LG; 954 L; 625 NOTE; 22 PB; 2 FIGURE; five modeled parts
evidence.summary: Verifies 940 main-poem lines, fourteen afterword verse lines, five stable editorial/intellectual parts, printed-page runs 58-74 and 360-364, and two omitted two-surface plate gaps in PB/REF sequencing.

evidence.citation: Walter Hübner, The Desert of Religion, Archiv 126 (1911), 58-74 and associated plates
evidence.summary: Direct edition evidence for title, first publication, editorial responsibility, witness key, base text, apparatus, 940-line text, manuscript descriptions, attribution history, afterword, and selected manuscript plates.

evidence.citation: K. Schreiner and Walter Hübner, The Desert of Religion, Archiv 126 (1911), 360-364
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for Schreiner's and Hübner's separate signed responsibilities, the A/C/S stemma, Add base choice, Northern language analysis, conditional Scotland suggestion, and initial source claims.

evidence.citation: Hope Emily Allen, The Desert of Religion: Addendum, Archiv 127 (1911), 388-390
evidence.summary: Maps extensive direct borrowing from the Speculum vitae and additional sources, qualifying the poem as a compilation while remaining downstream of the encoded 1911 edition.

evidence.citation: Anne Mouron, Praying with Images: Some Medieval Advice, The Way 55/1 (2016), 20-30
evidence.summary: Modern specialist evidence for the supplied title, anonymous author-compiler, fifteenth-century date, three Northern English illustrated witnesses, 943-line modern lineation, 22 sections, twenty tree diagrams, chief sources, and probable monastic but unproved Carthusian context.

Current official evidence for the base witness's exact ff. 46r-66v, Northern language, possible Carthusian production, date 1460-1500, full illustration inventory, Rolle locus, and NIMEV 672 identity.

evidence.citation: British Library catalog, Cotton MS Faustina B VI, part II, ff. 1-23
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for target ff. 3r-23v, NIMEV 672, 1425-1449 date, North England origin, incorporated Twelve Abuses and Rolle verses, and separate component identity.
Current official evidence for target ff. 10r-31v, DIMEV 1099, first-half-fifteenth-century date, illustrated content, altered quire order, and incorrect John Alcock attribution history.
Binds descriptive claims to the repository artifact, U-M encoding, two Hübner/Schreiner installments, three exact manuscript witnesses, complete journal scans, Allen addendum, and Mouron article.

open_questions.question: Why does Hübner's CME-encoded main text contain 940 lines while Mouron's modern edition lineation describes a 943-line poem?
next_steps: Consult Mouron's DPhil or completed EETS edition and compare its lineation against Hübner and all three witnesses.
Keep XML L counts, Hübner's printed lineation, visual-caption verse, and modern edited lineation distinct.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous author-compiler's date, exact origin, monastic audience, and possible Carthusian affiliation be demonstrated more precisely?
next_steps: Review Mouron's full edition and the linguistic and codicological literature for all three witnesses.
Separate work composition from each manuscript's copying place and institutional context.
Retain Walter Hilton, John Alcock, Richard Rolle, and 'follower of Rolle' only as disputed or contextual claims unless new authorship evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: How should a future corpus edition represent the poem's text, tree inscriptions, hermit captions, images, and witness-specific layouts without reducing the imagetext to Hübner's linear transcription?
next_steps: Obtain or regain complete color facsimiles of all three manuscripts.
Compare every textual and visual component against Mouron's modern edition and record witness-specific layouts and captions separately.
Do not infer absent image text from the CME XML or Hübner's two selected plates.

notes: The preferred title is an established modern editorial title, not a medieval rubric.

German editorial prose and Latin quotations are documented in the edition-facing content descriptions and lineage rather than exposed as work-level language facets; the medieval poem's language is Middle English.
The whole-work form remains verse even though the encoded edition includes substantial prose framing and criticism, because those are editorial layers rather than the medieval expression.
Richard Rolle appears as a depicted and quoted devotional authority and as the author of one source passage, not as secure author of the complete poem.
The three witnesses' dates, origins, foliation, and image rights remain lineage facts and are not flattened into one work-level date or region.
